en.title: Family Office Introduction in Switzerland: A Guide for Founders & Investors | GKP Insights
en.h1: Family Office Introduction in Switzerland: What It Is and How It Works
en.meta_description: A practical guide to family office introduction in Switzerland, how it differs from fundraising, who the key capital holders are, and how founders and asset owners can access Swiss private capital.
en.summary: Family office introduction is the trusted-channel alternative to public fundraising. This guide explains how it works in Switzerland's relationship-driven private capital market.

body.text: This article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute tax, legal, or investment advice. Readers should consult a qualified Swiss tax advisor, lawyer, or financial professional before making any decisions.

Switzerland hosts one of the world's most concentrated pools of private capital. Family offices, UHNWI investors, and institutional buyers collectively manage assets in the trillions of CHF, and a meaningful proportion is actively seeking off-market, relationship-sourced investment opportunities. For founders, entrepreneurs, and asset owners with compelling propositions, the challenge is reaching this capital through the right channel.

body.heading: What family office introduction means
body.text: Family office introduction is the practice of connecting a capital seeker (a company raising equity, a developer seeking project financing, an asset owner seeking a buyer or partner) with specific, qualified capital holders through a trusted intermediary. Unlike a public fundraising process or an investment roadshow, family office introduction is discreet, bilateral, and pre-qualified on both sides. The introducer acts as a trusted bridge: having verified the opportunity and the investor before making a connection.

body.heading: The Swiss private capital landscape
body.text: Switzerland's capital market for private deals operates through a distinct ecosystem. At the top are UHNWI individuals and single family offices, often managing assets from a successful business sale or multigenerational wealth, who invest directly in select opportunities. Below that are multi-family offices and investment platforms that aggregate capital from multiple wealthy families for co-investment. And adjacent to these are private banks that act as gatekeepers for their HNWI clients, selectively presenting opportunities to their network.

body.heading: How to approach Swiss private capital
body.text: The key cultural reality of Swiss private capital is this: it does not respond to cold outreach. Swiss UHNWIs and family offices are among the most privacy-conscious investors in the world. They engage with opportunities brought by people they trust. This means the route to Swiss private capital is almost always through an intermediary, and the quality of that intermediary, their standing with the relevant capital holders, and their reputation for bringing curated, pre-qualified opportunities is everything.

body.heading: What makes a family office introduction succeed
bullets: A clear, compelling investment thesis that speaks to the investor's known priorities
Complete and well-organised information. Swiss investors expect professionalism
Pre-qualification on both sides: the investor must be genuinely interested before the introduction is made
A trusted intermediary whose judgment the investor respects
Realistic valuation and structure expectations. Swiss capital is patient but disciplined

body.text: GKP works with a select number of mandates at any given time, focusing on opportunities where it has high conviction and where its network of Swiss and international private capital can add genuine value beyond the cheque. If you are considering a family office introduction mandate, GKP welcomes a confidential initial conversation.

faqs.q: How is family office introduction different from using an investment bank?
faqs.a: Investment banks typically run structured processes involving wide distribution of materials, roadshows, and book-building. Family office introduction through a boutique like GKP is targeted, discreet, and relationship-based, you reach fewer parties, but each one is specifically selected and pre-qualified. For private market transactions and where confidentiality matters, the boutique approach is typically superior.

faqs.q: What information do I need to prepare for a family office introduction mandate?
faqs.a: At minimum, you need a clear investment summary (2–4 pages), key financial data (historical and projected), a defined use of capital, and a realistic valuation or return expectation. For asset-backed mandates, supporting documentation on the asset. GKP works with you to ensure materials meet the standards expected by Swiss private investors.

faqs.q: What types of companies or opportunities does GKP work with for family office introduction?
faqs.a: GKP focuses on growth-stage companies with revenue traction and strategic appeal to private investors, real estate developers seeking equity partners, hospitality and lifestyle assets seeking capital or buyers, and select succession situations. We do not typically work with very early-stage ventures or mandates where generic VC or crowdfunding is a better fit.

de.title: Off-Market-Unternehmensübernahmen in der Schweiz: Was Käufer wissen müssen | GKP Insights
de.h1: Off-Market-Unternehmensübernahmen in der Schweiz: Der Leitfaden für Käufer
de.meta_description: Die Schweiz und der DACH-Raum erleben eine Nachfolgewelle mit Tausenden profitabler KMU, die den Besitzer wechseln. Dieser Leitfaden erklärt, wie qualifizierte Käufer Off-Market-Übernahmemöglichkeiten erschliessen, bevor sie öffentlich inseriert werden.
de.summary: Die Schweiz befindet sich mitten in einer bedeutenden Nachfolgewelle. Die besten Übernahmemöglichkeiten erreichen nie einen Makler. Hier erfahren Sie, wie man sie findet.

body.text: Dieser Artikel dient ausschliesslich Informationszwecken und stellt keine Steuer-, Rechts- oder Anlageberatung dar. Leser sollten vor jeglichen Entscheidungen einen qualifizierten Schweizer Steuerberater, Anwalt oder Finanzfachmann konsultieren.

Die Schweiz und der gesamte DACH-Raum befinden sich inmitten eines der grössten Vermögenstransferereignisse der Wirtschaftsgeschichte. Im nächsten Jahrzehnt werden Hunderttausende von inhabergeführten Unternehmen, viele davon hochprofitabel, tief verwurzelt und mit treuer Kundschaft, die über Jahrzehnte aufgebaut wurde, den Besitzer wechseln, während ihre Gründer das Rentenalter erreichen. Die attraktivsten dieser Übergänge erscheinen nie auf öffentlichen Plattformen.

body.heading: Die Schweizer KMU-Nachfolgelandschaft
body.text: Schweizer KMU bilden das Rückgrat der Wirtschaft, aber sie stehen vor einem akuten Nachfolgeproblem. Viele Gründer haben ihre Unternehmen über Jahrzehnte aufgebaut und haben keinen internen Nachfolger. Dies schafft eine Kategorie von Übergängen, die für den Eigentümer gleichzeitig dringend und für den Markt unsichtbar ist. Der Verkäufer möchte einen vertrauenswürdigen, diskreten Prozess. Der richtige Käufer, oft ein strategischer Erwerber oder eine Person mit Betriebserfahrung, ist ihm möglicherweise völlig unbekannt. Ein Vermittler überbrückt diese Lücke.

body.heading: Woher Off-Market-Übernahmen kommen
body.text: Off-Market-Unternehmensübernahmen in der Schweiz stammen typischerweise aus vier Quellen: dem eigenen professionellen Netzwerk des Verkäufers (Buchhalter, Anwälte, Treuhänder), sektorspezifischen Branchenbeziehungen, persönlichen Empfehlungen durch Unternehmensnetzwerke und vertrauenswürdigen Vermittlern, die aktive Sourcing-Gespräche mit Unternehmenseignern in relevanten Märkten pflegen.

body.heading: Was Käufer brauchen, um Off-Market-Übernahmen zu gewinnen
bullets: Ein klares Akquisitionsmandat: Branche, Grösse, Geografie, finanzielle Kriterien und was Sie über Kapital hinaus mitbringen
Klarheit über die vorher arrangierte Finanzierung. Schweizer Verkäufer schätzen Gewissheit
Geduld und Diskretion: der Zeitplan richtet sich nach der Bereitschaft des Verkäufers
Ein echtes Wertversprechen über den Preis hinaus. Schweizer Verkäufer kümmern sich oft tief um die Kontinuität ihres Unternehmens und ihrer Mitarbeitenden
Lokale Glaubwürdigkeit oder ein Partner, der diese bereitstellt

body.heading: Die Rolle eines vertrauenswürdigen Vermittlers
body.text: Ein effektiver Vermittler bei Schweizer Unternehmensübernahmen ist jemand, der von den Verkäufern, die er anspricht, wirklich bekannt und geschätzt wird, nicht jemand, der kalte E-Mails an Unternehmer im Auftrag eines Käufers sendet. GKP baut und pflegt langfristige Beziehungen zu Unternehmenseignern, Fachberatern und Branchennetzwerken in der ganzen Schweiz.

Wenn Sie ein qualifizierter Käufer. Einzelperson, Family Office oder Betriebsunternehmen, sind, der Off-Market-Übernahmemöglichkeiten in der Schweiz sucht, freut sich GKP auf ein vertrauliches Gespräch über Ihre Kriterien.

faqs.q: Wie viele Schweizer Unternehmen durchlaufen in den nächsten 10 Jahren eine Nachfolge?
faqs.a: Schweizer Unternehmensverbände schätzen, dass Zehntausende von Schweizer KMU im nächsten Jahrzehnt einen Eigentümerwechsel erfahren werden, angetrieben durch Gründerruhestand. In Deutschland liegt die Zahl in den Hunderttausenden. Die Mehrheit dieser Unternehmen wird einen diskreten, beziehungsbasierten Übergang einem öffentlichen Verkaufsprozess vorziehen.

faqs.q: Welche Branchen haben die meisten nachfolgebedingten Übernahmemöglichkeiten in der Schweiz?
faqs.a: Branchen mit der meisten Aktivität umfassen Hospitality (Hotels, Restaurants), Business Services (Buchhaltung, Beratung, IT-Dienstleistungen), Fertigung und Präzisionstechnik, Gesundheitsdienstleistungen und professionelle Praktiken. Luxuseinzelhandel und Nischen-Konsumgüter produzieren ebenfalls regelmässige Nachfolgesituationen.

faqs.q: Können internationale Käufer GKP nutzen, um Schweizer Übernahmeziele zu finden?
faqs.a: Ja. GKP arbeitet regelmässig mit internationalen Käufern aus Europa, dem Golf und Asien zusammen, die Schweizer Übernahmeziele suchen. Wir bieten lokalen Marktzugang, Beziehungsglaubwürdigkeit und Vermittlungsunterstützung während des gesamten Prozesses.

faqs.q: Berechnet GKP für Käufermandat-Akquisitionsbeschaffung?
faqs.a: GKP berechnet typischerweise eine Erfolgsgebühr beim Abschluss einer Transaktion, strukturiert gemäss dem Mandatswert und der Komplexität. Bei komplexen, langdauernden Mandaten kann ein Retainer anfallen. Alle Gebührenstrukturen werden im Voraus transparent vereinbart.

en.title: Off-Market Business Acquisitions in Switzerland: What Buyers Need to Know | GKP Insights
en.h1: Off-Market Business Acquisitions in Switzerland: The Buyer's Guide
en.meta_description: The Swiss and DACH region is undergoing a succession wave with thousands of profitable SMEs changing hands. This guide explains how qualified buyers can access off-market acquisition opportunities before they are publicly listed.
en.summary: Switzerland is in the middle of a significant succession wave. The best acquisition opportunities never reach a broker. Here is how to find them.

body.text: This article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute tax, legal, or investment advice. Readers should consult a qualified Swiss tax advisor, lawyer, or financial professional before making any decisions.

Switzerland and the wider DACH region are in the midst of one of the largest wealth transfer events in economic history. Over the next decade, hundreds of thousands of owner-operated businesses, many of them highly profitable, deeply established, and with loyal client bases built over decades, will change hands as their founders reach retirement age. The most attractive of these transitions never appear on public platforms. They are handled privately, through trusted relationships, by buyers who were in the right place at the right time.

body.heading: The Swiss SME succession landscape
body.text: Swiss SMEs are the backbone of the economy, but they face an acute succession problem. Many founders built their businesses over decades and have no internal successor. Their children may have chosen different paths. Existing management may lack the capital or appetite for a buy-out. This creates a category of transition that is simultaneously urgent for the owner and invisible to the market. The seller wants a trusted, discreet process. The right buyer, often a strategic acquirer or an individual with operating experience, may be entirely unknown to them. An intermediary bridges that gap.

body.heading: Where off-market acquisitions come from
body.text: Off-market business acquisitions in Switzerland typically originate from four sources: the seller's own professional network (accountants, lawyers, fiduciaries), sector-specific industry relationships, personal introductions through business networks, and trusted intermediaries who maintain active sourcing conversations with business owners across relevant markets. The last category, trusted intermediaries, is where most off-market deal flow comes from for buyers who lack deep local roots.

body.heading: What buyers need to succeed in off-market acquisitions
bullets: A clear acquisition mandate: industry, size, geography, financial criteria, and what you bring beyond capital
Pre-arranged financing clarity. Swiss sellers value certainty and do not want to run a process twice
Patience and discretion: the timeline is set by the seller's readiness, not the buyer's urgency
A genuine value proposition beyond price. Swiss sellers often care deeply about the continuity of their business and staff
Local credibility or a partner who provides it

body.heading: The role of a trusted intermediary
body.text: An effective intermediary in Swiss business acquisitions is one who is genuinely known and trusted by the sellers they approach, not one who sends cold emails to business owners on behalf of a buyer. GKP builds and maintains long-term relationships with business owners, professional advisors, and industry networks across Switzerland. When a situation arises, GKP knows who the right buyers are and can approach them with a credible, professional introduction.

If you are a qualified buyer, individual, family office, or operating company, seeking off-market acquisition opportunities in Switzerland, GKP would welcome a confidential conversation about your criteria.

faqs.q: How many Swiss businesses are going through succession in the next 10 years?
faqs.a: Swiss business associations estimate that tens of thousands of Swiss SMEs will undergo ownership transition over the next decade, driven by founder retirement. In Germany, the figure is in the hundreds of thousands. The majority of these businesses will seek a discreet, relationship-based transition rather than a public sale process.

faqs.q: What industries have the most succession-driven acquisition opportunities in Switzerland?
faqs.a: Industries with the most activity include hospitality (hotels, restaurants), business services (accounting, consulting, IT services), manufacturing and precision engineering, healthcare services, and professional practices. Luxury retail and niche consumer goods also produce regular succession situations.

faqs.q: Can international buyers use GKP to find Swiss acquisition targets?
faqs.a: Yes. GKP regularly works with international buyers, from Europe, the Gulf, and Asia, seeking Swiss acquisition targets. We provide local market access, relationship credibility, and intermediary support throughout the process. Understanding of Swiss cultural and commercial norms is essential for successful acquisitions, and GKP provides this context.

faqs.a: GKP typically charges a success fee upon completion of a transaction, structured according to the mandate value and complexity. For complex, long-duration mandates, a retainer may apply. All fee structures are agreed transparently in advance.

en.title: Lex Koller Explained: A Guide for Asian Investors Buying Swiss Real Estate | GKP
en.h1: Lex Koller Explained: What Asian Investors Need to Know Before Buying Swiss Real Estate
en.meta_description: A clear guide to Switzerland's Lex Koller law for Singapore, Hong Kong, Malaysian, and Thai investors, what it restricts, what it permits, and how qualified buyers navigate it.
en.summary: Switzerland's Lex Koller law is the primary legal consideration for Asian investors seeking Swiss real estate. This guide explains what it restricts, what it permits, and how qualified buyers from Singapore, Hong Kong, Malaysia, and beyond can access the market.

body.text: This article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute tax, legal, or investment advice. Readers should consult a qualified Swiss tax advisor, lawyer, or financial professional before making any decisions.

Switzerland is one of the world's most sought-after real estate markets for Asian private wealth. But before any transaction can proceed, one piece of legislation demands attention: the Federal Act on the Acquisition of Real Estate by Persons Abroad, universally known as Lex Koller. Understanding it upfront saves time, avoids missteps, and opens up the right structuring pathways.

body.heading: What is Lex Koller?
body.text: Lex Koller (formally the Bundesgesetz über den Erwerb von Grundstücken durch Personen im Ausland, or BewG) was introduced in 1983 to limit foreign ownership of Swiss residential real estate. Its core principle: non-Swiss residents generally require a government permit to purchase residential property in Switzerland. The law applies to individuals and legal entities where the ultimate beneficial owner is a non-Swiss resident.

body.heading: What Lex Koller restricts
body.text: The law restricts the purchase of residential real estate by persons domiciled abroad. This includes apartments, houses, and chalets intended for private use. The restriction applies regardless of nationality, a Singapore, Hong Kong, or Malaysian passport holder living outside Switzerland faces the same rules as any other foreign non-resident buyer.

body.heading: What Lex Koller does NOT restrict
bullets: Commercial real estate, office buildings, retail, warehouses, and mixed-use properties with commercial ground floors, is freely purchasable by foreign buyers with no permit required
Residential property purchased by foreign nationals who are domiciled in Switzerland (i.e., hold a Swiss residence permit) is also exempt
Certain hotel and resort properties may qualify as commercial, depending on their legal classification
Corporate acquisitions that are primarily commercial in nature may not trigger Lex Koller even if residential components are present

body.heading: Cantonal quotas: the residential pathway
body.text: For holiday or vacation properties in designated Swiss resort cantons. Valais, Graubünden, Bern Oberland, and others, foreign buyers may acquire residential property within cantonal annual quotas. These permits are limited in number, issued by cantonal authorities, and subject to conditions (the property must be used as a holiday residence and may not be let for extended periods). Properties in Zurich, Geneva, Zug, and Basel as primary urban centres do not fall under this quota system and are generally not accessible to non-resident foreign buyers for residential use.

body.heading: Practical implications for Asian buyers
body.text: For most Singapore, Hong Kong, Malaysian, and Thai investors approaching the Swiss market as non-residents, the most straightforward path is commercial real estate, which carries no Lex Koller restriction. For those seeking residential property, the options are: establishing Swiss residency (which opens the residential market fully), acquiring within cantonal holiday property quotas in designated resort areas, or purchasing via a structure where the commercial use classification is defensible. All of these pathways require specialist Swiss legal counsel.

body.heading: Global Key Partners' approach
body.text: GKP does not provide legal advice. What Global Key Partners does is ensure that Asian investors approaching the Swiss market understand the Lex Koller framework before beginning a property search, and that they are connected with the right specialist legal counsel from the outset. We work alongside experienced Swiss property lawyers on every international buyer mandate, structuring comes first, search comes second.

faqs.q: Does Lex Koller apply to Singapore investors buying Swiss property?
faqs.a: Yes. Lex Koller applies to all non-Swiss residents purchasing residential real estate in Switzerland, regardless of nationality. Singapore investors domiciled outside Switzerland require a permit to purchase Swiss residential property. Commercial real estate is not subject to Lex Koller and may be purchased freely.

faqs.q: Can a Hong Kong investor buy a chalet in the Swiss Alps?
faqs.a: In designated resort cantons (Valais, Graubünden, parts of Bern Oberland), foreign non-resident buyers may purchase holiday chalets within annual cantonal quotas. These quotas are limited and subject to conditions. For most urban residential property in Zurich, Geneva, or Zug, non-resident foreign buyers cannot purchase under current Lex Koller rules without first establishing Swiss residency.

faqs.q: What is the easiest way for an Asian investor to access Swiss residential real estate?
faqs.a: Establishing Swiss residency, which is achievable for qualifying high-net-worth individuals, removes the Lex Koller restriction entirely. Alternatively, focusing on commercial real estate (which carries no restriction) or qualifying Alpine resort properties (within cantonal quotas) are the main pathways for non-resident buyers. GKP connects clients with specialist legal counsel to assess the most appropriate route.

faqs.q: Does Lex Koller affect corporate buyers?
faqs.a: Yes. Lex Koller applies to legal entities where the controlling beneficial owner is a non-Swiss resident. Acquiring through a Swiss company does not automatically circumvent the restriction if the ultimate beneficial owner is a foreign non-resident. Specialist legal advice on structuring is essential.

Dieser Leitfaden erklärt, was es einschränkt, was es erlaubt und wie qualifizierte Käufer aus Singapur, Hongkong, Malaysia und darüber hinaus den Markt erschliessen können.

body.text: Dieser Artikel dient ausschliesslich Informationszwecken und stellt keine Steuer-, Rechts- oder Anlageberatung dar. Leser sollten vor jeglichen Entscheidungen einen qualifizierten Schweizer Steuerberater, Anwalt oder Finanzfachmann konsultieren.

Die Schweiz ist einer der begehrtesten Immobilienmärkte der Welt für asiatisches Privatvermögen. Aber bevor eine Transaktion voranschreiten kann, erfordert ein Gesetz Aufmerksamkeit: das Bundesgesetz über den Erwerb von Grundstücken durch Personen im Ausland, allgemein bekannt als Lex Koller.

body.heading: Was ist Lex Koller?
body.text: Lex Koller wurde 1983 eingeführt, um den ausländischen Erwerb von Schweizer Wohnimmobilien zu begrenzen. Sein Kernprinzip: Nicht in der Schweiz ansässige Personen benötigen grundsätzlich eine behördliche Bewilligung für den Erwerb von Wohnimmobilien in der Schweiz.

body.heading: Was Lex Koller NICHT einschränkt
bullets: Gewerbeimmobilien. Bürogebäude, Detailhandel, Lagerhäuser und gemischt genutzte Objekte, können von ausländischen Käufern ohne Bewilligung frei erworben werden
Wohnimmobilien, die von in der Schweiz domizilierten Ausländern (d.h, mit Schweizer Aufenthaltsbewilligung) erworben werden, sind ebenfalls ausgenommen
Bestimmte Hotel- und Resortimmobilien können je nach rechtlicher Klassifizierung als gewerblich gelten

body.heading: Praktische Auswirkungen für asiatische Käufer
body.text: Für die meisten Investoren aus Singapur, Hongkong, Malaysia und Thailand ist der unkomplizierteste Weg Gewerbeimmobilien, für die keine Lex-Koller-Beschränkung gilt. Für diejenigen, die Wohnimmobilien suchen, sind die Optionen: Schweizer Wohnsitz begründen, Erwerb im Rahmen kantonaler Ferienwohnungskontingente in designierten Resortgebieten oder Kauf über eine Struktur mit vertretbarer gewerblicher Nutzungsklassifizierung.

faqs.q: Gilt Lex Koller für Singapurer Investoren beim Kauf Schweizer Immobilien?
faqs.a: Ja. Lex Koller gilt für alle nicht in der Schweiz ansässigen Käufer von Schweizer Wohnimmobilien, unabhängig von der Nationalität. Gewerbeimmobilien unterliegen nicht Lex Koller und können frei erworben werden.

faqs.q: Kann ein Hongkonger Investor ein Chalet in den Schweizer Alpen kaufen?
faqs.a: In designierten Resortgebieten können ausländische Nicht-Residenten Ferienchalets im Rahmen jährlicher kantonaler Kontingente erwerben. Diese Kontingente sind begrenzt und an Bedingungen geknüpft. Für städtische Wohnimmobilien in Zürich, Genf oder Zug gilt dies nicht.

fr.title: La Lex Koller Expliquée : Guide pour les Investisseurs Asiatiques | GKP
fr.h1: La Lex Koller Expliquée : Ce que les Investisseurs Asiatiques Doivent Savoir Avant d'Acheter de l'Immobilier Suisse
fr.meta_description: Un guide clair sur la loi suisse Lex Koller pour les investisseurs de Singapour, Hong Kong, de Malaisie et de Thaïlande, ce qu'elle restreint, ce qu'elle permet et comment les acheteurs qualifiés la naviguent.
fr.summary: La Lex Koller suisse est la principale considération juridique pour les investisseurs asiatiques cherchant de l'immobilier suisse. Ce guide explique ce qu'elle restreint, ce qu'elle permet et comment les acheteurs qualifiés de Singapour, Hong Kong, de Malaisie et d'ailleurs peuvent accéder au marché.

body.text: La Suisse est l'un des marchés immobiliers les plus recherchés au monde par le patrimoine privé asiatique. Mais avant toute transaction, une législation mérite attention : la Loi fédérale sur l'acquisition d'immeubles par des personnes à l'étranger, universellement connue sous le nom de Lex Koller.

body.heading: Qu'est-ce que la Lex Koller ?
body.text: Introduite en 1983 pour limiter la propriété étrangère de l'immobilier résidentiel suisse, son principe central est que les non-résidents suisses ont généralement besoin d'une autorisation gouvernementale pour acheter des propriétés résidentielles en Suisse.

body.heading: Ce que la Lex Koller ne restreint PAS
bullets: L'immobilier commercial, immeubles de bureaux, commerces, entrepôts et propriétés à usage mixte, peut être acheté librement par des acheteurs étrangers sans autorisation
L'immobilier résidentiel acheté par des ressortissants étrangers domiciliés en Suisse est également exempté
Certaines propriétés hôtelières et de resort peuvent être qualifiées de commerciales selon leur classification juridique

body.heading: Implications pratiques pour les acheteurs asiatiques
body.text: Pour la plupart des investisseurs de Singapour, Hong Kong, de Malaisie et de Thaïlande approchant le marché suisse comme non-résidents, la voie la plus simple est l'immobilier commercial, qui ne comporte aucune restriction Lex Koller. Pour ceux cherchant de l'immobilier résidentiel, les options sont : établir la résidence suisse, acquérir dans les quotas cantonaux de propriétés de vacances en zones de resort désignées, ou acheter via une structure où la classification à usage commercial est défendable.

faqs.q: La Lex Koller s'applique-t-elle aux investisseurs singapouriens achetant de l'immobilier suisse ?
faqs.a: Oui. La Lex Koller s'applique à tous les non-résidents suisses achetant de l'immobilier résidentiel en Suisse, quelle que soit leur nationalité. L'immobilier commercial n'est pas soumis à la Lex Koller et peut être acheté librement.

faqs.q: Quelle est la façon la plus simple pour un investisseur asiatique d'accéder à l'immobilier résidentiel suisse ?
faqs.a: Établir la résidence suisse, accessible pour les particuliers à haute valeur nette qualifiés, supprime entièrement la restriction Lex Koller. Alternativement, se concentrer sur l'immobilier commercial ou les propriétés de vacances alpines qualifiées sont les principales voies pour les acheteurs non-résidents.

en.title: How Singapore, Hong Kong & Malaysian Family Offices Are Investing in Switzerland | GKP
en.h1: How Singapore, Hong Kong, and Malaysian Family Offices Are Investing in Switzerland
en.meta_description: An overview of how Asian family offices, particularly from Singapore, Hong Kong, and Malaysia, are approaching Swiss real estate, private markets, and business acquisitions in 2025 and beyond.
en.summary: Asian family offices are among the most active allocators to Swiss private markets. This piece examines how family offices from Singapore, Hong Kong, and Malaysia are approaching Switzerland, what they seek, how they access it, and what GKP observes from its position in the market.

body.text: This article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute tax, legal, or investment advice. Readers should consult a qualified Swiss tax advisor, lawyer, or financial professional before making any decisions.

The flow of Asian private capital into Switzerland is not new, but it is accelerating. Singapore in particular has emerged as the primary booking and decision-making hub for Southeast Asian private wealth, and its family offices are increasingly active in Swiss real estate, business acquisitions, and co-investment opportunities. GKP, operating from Zug, observes this flow directly.

body.heading: Why Switzerland, why now
body.text: Three converging factors are driving increased Asian family office interest in Switzerland. First, Switzerland's political and legal stability is increasingly valued against a backdrop of regional geopolitical uncertainty. Second, Swiss private market valuations remain more attractive than comparable assets in London or New York. Third, the weakening Swiss franc in recent years has created an entry window for USD- and SGD-denominated capital. For family offices managing multi-generational wealth, Switzerland represents a core allocation, not a trade.

body.heading: What Singapore family offices seek
body.text: Singapore family offices approaching Switzerland typically look for a combination of capital preservation and moderate yield. Off-market residential real estate in Zurich and Zug is the most common entry point, particularly for principals already familiar with Switzerland through business or personal connections. Co-investment alongside Swiss operators in hospitality or real estate development is a growing secondary interest. A smaller but significant subset is actively seeking Swiss business acquisitions, particularly succession situations in healthcare, precision manufacturing, and professional services.

body.heading: Hong Kong: portfolio restructuring into Europe
body.text: Hong Kong family offices have been among the most active restructurers of geographic exposure over the past five years. Switzerland, along with the broader Alpine region, has absorbed a meaningful portion of this capital. The Swiss market's combination of legal certainty, discretion, and the absence of exchange controls makes it particularly attractive for capital seeking a long-term European anchor. GKP works with Hong Kong-based principals seeking off-market Swiss real estate and business introduction mandates.

body.heading: Malaysian investors: the European education connection
body.text: Malaysia's UHNWI community has longstanding ties to European institutions and business culture, many principals or their children have studied in Switzerland, the UK, or broader Europe. This creates a different entry dynamic: Malaysian investors often come to the Swiss market with existing relationships and clearer criteria. GKP supports this group with off-market real estate access, capital introduction, and business acquisition introductions tailored to their existing familiarity with the market.

body.heading: How the access actually works
body.text: For all three groups, the common thread is the need for a trusted Swiss-side partner with genuine relationships. Access to off-market Swiss real estate, family office introductions, and private business deal flow does not come from online platforms or public listings, it comes from embedded local relationships. This is GKP's core position: permanently present in Switzerland, trusted by Swiss asset owners, and experienced in bridging the expectations and conventions of both sides of the transaction.

faqs.q: Which Asian family offices are most active in Swiss real estate?
faqs.a: Singapore-based family offices are currently the most active Asian buyers in the Swiss private real estate market, followed by Hong Kong and Malaysian principals. Many operate through Singapore-domiciled structures regardless of the principal's nationality, reflecting Singapore's role as the region's primary wealth booking centre.

faqs.q: What is the minimum investment size for Swiss real estate relevant to family offices?
faqs.a: In GKP's experience, off-market Swiss residential and commercial real estate mandates from family offices typically start at CHF 2–3 million for residential and CHF 5 million and above for commercial or hospitality assets. These are not absolute minimums: they reflect the practical threshold at which discreet off-market introductions make economic sense relative to public market alternatives.

faqs.q: How do Asian family offices structure Swiss real estate acquisitions?
faqs.a: Structuring depends on the asset type and the buyer's domicile. Commercial real estate requires no Lex Koller permit and can be held directly or through a Swiss AG or GmbH. Residential real estate for non-Swiss-resident buyers is subject to Lex Koller restrictions, structuring options include Swiss residency establishment, cantonal holiday quota acquisitions, or commercial classification where applicable. GKP works with specialist Swiss legal counsel on every international buyer mandate.

faqs.q: Can GKP introduce Asian family offices to Swiss investment opportunities directly?
faqs.a: Yes. GKP works with qualified Asian family offices on a mandate basis, understanding the investment criteria, financial profile, and objectives, then actively searching our Swiss network for relevant off-market opportunities. We make introductions only when there is a credible match on both sides.

body.text: Dieser Artikel dient ausschliesslich Informationszwecken und stellt keine Steuer-, Rechts- oder Anlageberatung dar. Leser sollten vor jeglichen Entscheidungen einen qualifizierten Schweizer Steuerberater, Anwalt oder Finanzfachmann konsultieren.

Der Fluss asiatischen Privatkapitals in die Schweiz ist nicht neu, aber er beschleunigt sich. Singapur hat sich insbesondere als primärer Buchungs- und Entscheidungshub für südostasiatisches Privatvermögen etabliert, und seine Family Offices sind zunehmend aktiv in Schweizer Immobilien, Unternehmensübernahmen und Co-Investmentgelegenheiten.

body.heading: Warum die Schweiz, warum jetzt
body.text: Drei konvergierende Faktoren treiben das gestiegene Interesse asiatischer Family Offices an der Schweiz. Erstens wird die politische und rechtliche Stabilität der Schweiz angesichts regionaler geopolitischer Unsicherheit zunehmend geschätzt. Drittens hat der geschwächte Schweizer Franken ein Einstiegsfenster für in USD und SGD denominiertes Kapital geschaffen.

body.heading: Was Singapurer Family Offices suchen
body.text: Singapurer Family Offices, die die Schweiz angehen, suchen typischerweise eine Kombination aus Kapitalerhalt und moderatem Ertrag. Off-Market-Wohnimmobilien in Zürich und Zug sind der häufigste Einstiegspunkt. Co-Investments neben Schweizer Betreibern in Hospitality oder Immobilienentwicklung sind ein wachsendes sekundäres Interesse.

faqs.q: Welche asiatischen Family Offices sind am aktivsten im Schweizer Immobilienmarkt?
faqs.a: Singapurbasierte Family Offices sind derzeit die aktivsten asiatischen Käufer im Schweizer Privatimmobilienmarkt, gefolgt von Hongkonger und malaysischen Principals.

faqs.q: Kann GKP asiatische Family Offices direkt mit Schweizer Investitionsmöglichkeiten verbinden?
faqs.a: Ja. GKP arbeitet mit qualifizierten asiatischen Family Offices auf Mandatsbasis, versteht die Investitionskriterien und sucht aktiv im Schweizer Netzwerk von GKP nach relevanten Off-Market-Gelegenheiten.

en.title: Gulf & Middle East Investors: How to Access Swiss Real Estate and European Private Markets | GKP
en.h1: How Gulf and Middle East Investors Access Swiss Real Estate and European Private Markets
en.meta_description: A guide for UAE, Saudi, and Gulf family offices seeking off-market Swiss real estate and European private investment opportunities, how the market works and how GKP provides access.
en.summary: Gulf and Middle East private capital has long had an affinity with Switzerland. This guide explains how UAE, Saudi, and broader Gulf family offices are approaching Swiss real estate and European private markets, and what access looks like in practice.

body.text: This article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute tax, legal, or investment advice. Readers should consult a qualified Swiss tax advisor, lawyer, or financial professional before making any decisions.

Switzerland and the Gulf have maintained a deep financial relationship for decades. Swiss private banks, wealth managers, and real estate markets have been among the preferred destinations for Middle Eastern capital, from sovereign wealth to family office to individual UHNWI. What has changed in recent years is the sophistication of the approach: Gulf family offices are no longer passive depositors but active allocators, seeking direct real estate, co-investment, and business acquisition opportunities in Switzerland and Europe.

body.heading: Why Switzerland remains a core Gulf allocation
body.text: Switzerland offers Gulf investors a combination that is hard to replicate: genuine political neutrality (valued particularly by investors from countries with complex geopolitical relationships), robust rule of law, no exchange controls, strong asset discretion, and one of Europe's most liquid and supply-constrained real estate markets. For UAE and Saudi family offices managing long-horizon wealth, Swiss real estate is not a trade, it is an anchor position.

body.heading: Dubai: the Gulf's access hub for European investment
body.text: Dubai has increasingly become the coordination point for Gulf capital flowing into Europe. Many of the region's most active family offices, single-family offices, and UHNWI investors are based or incorporated in the UAE, using Dubai's financial infrastructure to manage European allocations. GKP works with UAE-based principals seeking Swiss and European off-market opportunities, connecting them with its embedded Swiss-side relationships.

body.heading: What Gulf investors seek in Switzerland
bullets: Off-market residential real estate in prime Swiss cities, particularly Zurich, Geneva, and Zug, as a long-term store of value
Commercial real estate yielding stable, CHF-denominated returns (not subject to Lex Koller restrictions)
Business acquisition introductions: particularly in succession situations in Swiss SMEs
Hospitality assets in Switzerland and the Alps, with operational upside potential
Co-investment alongside Swiss-based operators in real estate development or private equity

body.heading: Lex Koller for Gulf buyers
body.text: As with all non-Swiss-resident foreign buyers, Gulf investors face Switzerland's Lex Koller restrictions on residential real estate purchases. Commercial property, including office buildings, retail, and certain hospitality assets, carries no such restriction and can be purchased freely by foreign investors. For residential property, the pathways are Swiss residency establishment, cantonal holiday property quotas in designated Alpine resort areas, or commercial-classification structures. GKP connects Gulf clients with specialist Swiss legal counsel on all structuring questions from the outset.

body.heading: The two-way opportunity
body.text: The relationship is not one-directional. GKP also supports Swiss and European companies seeking to establish relationships, find distribution partners, or access capital in the Gulf. Our founders' international experience, spanning Switzerland, Southeast Asia, and global private markets, positions us to bridge both sides of the Switzerland–Gulf corridor.

faqs.q: Can UAE investors buy Swiss real estate without restrictions?
faqs.a: UAE investors who are non-Swiss residents can purchase Swiss commercial real estate without restriction. Residential real estate is subject to Lex Koller, requiring either Swiss residency, a cantonal holiday property quota permit, or a structure where commercial classification applies. GKP connects Gulf clients with specialist Swiss legal counsel on these structuring questions.

faqs.q: What types of Swiss real estate are most popular with Gulf family offices?
faqs.a: Gulf family offices typically focus on prime residential real estate in Zurich and Geneva (often purchased after establishing Swiss residency), commercial real estate for yield, and Alpine hospitality assets. The preference is generally for established markets with strong liquidity and clear legal frameworks.

faqs.q: Does GKP work with Saudi or Qatari investors, not just UAE?
faqs.a: Yes. GKP works with qualified investors from across the Gulf region. UAE, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Kuwait, and Bahrain. Many structure their European allocations through UAE-based entities, but GKP has no geographic restriction on the origin of client capital, provided all applicable compliance requirements are met.

faqs.q: How confidential is the process with GKP?
faqs.a: All GKP mandates are handled with Swiss-standard confidentiality. Client identity, transaction intent, and opportunity specifics are never disclosed without explicit consent from all parties. For Gulf-based investors who value transacting through trusted, discreet intermediaries, this approach is both familiar and essential.

Die Schweiz und der Golf unterhalten seit Jahrzehnten eine tiefe Finanzbeziehung. Schweizer Privatbanken, Vermögensverwalter und Immobilienmärkte gehören zu den bevorzugten Zielen für nahöstliches Kapital, von Staatsfonds über Family Offices bis hin zu vermögenden Privatpersonen.

body.heading: Warum die Schweiz eine Kernallokation für den Golf bleibt
body.text: Die Schweiz bietet Golfinvestoren eine schwer replizierbare Kombination: echte politische Neutralität, Rechtsstaatlichkeit, keine Devisenkontrollen, starke Vermögensdiskretion und einen der liquidesten und angebotsknappen Immobilienmärkte Europas.

body.heading: Lex Koller für Golfkäufer

faqs.q: Arbeitet GKP auch mit saudischen oder katarischen Investoren?

en.title: Investing in Southeast Asia from Switzerland and Europe: A Guide for Private Investors | GKP
en.h1: Investing in Southeast Asia from Switzerland and Europe: What Private Investors Need to Know
en.meta_description: A practical guide for Swiss and European family offices, private investors, and capital funds considering investment in Southeast Asia, real estate, hospitality, private equity, and how to access the market.
en.summary: Southeast Asia represents one of the most compelling investment frontiers for Swiss and European private capital. This guide examines what Swiss and European investors are targeting in the region, and how GKP facilitates access from its position across both markets.

body.text: This article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute tax, legal, or investment advice. Readers should consult a qualified Swiss tax advisor, lawyer, or financial professional before making any decisions.

For Swiss and European family offices and private investors, Southeast Asia has moved from a peripheral consideration to a core portfolio question. A combination of demographic tailwinds, urbanisation, rising middle-class consumption, and government-driven economic development across Singapore, Thailand, Malaysia, and Vietnam has created an investment landscape that offers both growth exposure and, in select segments, yield that European markets have not delivered in years.

body.heading: Why Southeast Asia now
body.text: Several structural factors are converging to make Southeast Asia attractive for European private capital in the mid-2020s. First, valuations in hospitality, real estate, and private businesses remain genuinely attractive relative to comparable European or US assets. Second, the region's middle class is expanding faster than anywhere outside South Asia, creating sustained domestic consumption growth that underpins returns. Third, Singapore's emergence as a global wealth management hub has made the region more legible and accessible for international capital. And fourth, the post-COVID rebound in tourism, particularly in Thailand, Bali, and Vietnam, has reset hospitality investment fundamentals significantly upward.

body.heading: What Swiss and European investors are targeting
bullets: Hospitality and resort real estate: boutique hotels, branded residences, and luxury villa developments in Thailand (Koh Samui, Phuket, Hua Hin), Bali, and Vietnam
Commercial real estate in Singapore: the region's most liquid, transparent, and institutionally governed property market
Private equity co-investment alongside experienced regional operators in consumer, healthcare, and logistics sectors
Business acquisitions and minority stakes in profitable, owner-managed regional businesses approaching succession or seeking growth capital
Real estate development: off-plan investments in resort markets with strong operator backing

body.heading: Thailand: the hospitality opportunity
body.text: Thailand remains the most accessible Southeast Asian market for Swiss and European private investors seeking hospitality exposure. Bangkok's luxury residential and commercial real estate market is increasingly institutional; but it is the resort markets. Koh Samui, Phuket, Hua Hin, and Chiang Mai, that offer the most compelling entry points for private capital. GKP facilitates introductions to vetted Thai hospitality opportunities for qualified Swiss and European investors, working through its founders' direct networks in the region.

body.heading: Singapore: the gateway and destination
body.text: Singapore serves a dual function for Swiss and European investors: it is both the regional gateway, the most stable, transparent, and legally robust jurisdiction through which to access Southeast Asian deals, and itself an investment destination, with commercial real estate, family office co-investment, and private equity opportunities that attract European capital directly. GKP works with Swiss and European clients on Singapore-side introductions alongside its broader regional mandate.

body.heading: Malaysia and Vietnam: emerging private market opportunities
body.text: Malaysia's Kuala Lumpur market offers a combination of relatively affordable commercial real estate, a large English-speaking professional class, and growing private equity deal flow in consumer and technology sectors. Vietnam. Hanoi and Ho Chi Minh City: is the region's highest-growth story, with private investors increasingly active in manufacturing, logistics, and consumer real estate. Both markets require experienced local partners; GKP facilitates the introductions.

body.heading: How Global Key Partners bridges Switzerland and Southeast Asia
body.text: GKP is headquartered in Zug, Switzerland, with direct professional networks across Singapore, Thailand, and the broader Southeast Asian ecosystem, built through our founders' years of professional experience in the region. We do not operate as a regional fund or a mass-market platform. We facilitate specific, qualified introductions: Swiss or European capital with clear criteria, matched with vetted regional opportunities sourced through trusted relationships on the ground.

faqs.q: What are the main risks for Swiss investors in Southeast Asia?
faqs.a: The main risks include regulatory and legal uncertainty in some markets (particularly land ownership restrictions for foreigners in Thailand and Vietnam), currency exposure relative to CHF, political risk in less stable jurisdictions, and liquidity risk, exit timelines for private market investments can be longer than in European markets. GKP addresses these by working only with trusted, experienced local partners and focusing on markets and asset types where the risk/return profile is defensible.

faqs.q: Can Swiss or European investors own real estate directly in Thailand?
faqs.a: Thai property law restricts direct foreign ownership of land but allows foreign ownership of condominium units (up to 49% of a building's total area). Resort real estate investments typically involve long-term leasehold structures (30+30 years) or Thai company ownership structures. GKP connects investors with specialist local legal counsel on all structuring questions before any investment commitment.

faqs.q: Is Singapore real estate accessible to Swiss investors?
faqs.a: Yes. Singapore commercial real estate is freely purchasable by foreign investors with no ownership restrictions. Residential property is subject to Additional Buyer's Stamp Duty (ABSD) for foreign buyers, which has increased significantly in recent years, making commercial real estate and co-investment vehicles the more common entry points for Swiss and European private capital.

en.title: How to Buy a Business in Switzerland as a Foreign Investor | GKP
en.h1: How to Buy a Business in Switzerland as a Foreign Investor
en.meta_description: A practical guide for foreign investors, family offices, and strategic buyers on acquiring Swiss businesses, succession situations, SMEs, and off-market M&A. What to know, what to expect, and how GKP helps.
en.summary: Switzerland's SME landscape is one of Europe's most attractive for strategic acquirers, but off-market access, legal structuring, and cultural nuance make foreign acquisitions complex. This guide covers what foreign investors need to know before approaching the Swiss M&A market.

body.text: This article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute tax, legal, or investment advice. Readers should consult a qualified Swiss tax advisor, lawyer, or financial professional before making any decisions.

Switzerland is home to over 600,000 SMEs, the vast majority owner-operated and family-owned. A significant portion will change hands in the next decade as the founder generation retires, and many of these transitions will happen quietly, without ever appearing on a public M&A platform. For foreign investors and family offices, this represents one of Europe's most compelling private acquisition opportunities. But accessing it requires more than capital.

body.heading: Why Switzerland's SME Market Attracts Foreign Buyers
body.text: Swiss SMEs are disproportionately strong: high margins, stable cash flows, loyal workforces, and global niches in precision manufacturing, professional services, technology, healthcare, and speciality retail. The Swiss brand carries commercial weight. A Swiss-headquartered company opens doors, in Asia, the Middle East, and across Europe, that a comparable German or Austrian company cannot.

body.heading: The Succession Opportunity
body.text: Switzerland faces a structural succession wave. An estimated 70,000–90,000 Swiss SMEs are expected to change ownership in the coming decade due to founder retirement. Many owners have no family successor, no appetite for a public sale process, and no desire to approach a trade buyer or private equity firm. They want a trusted handover: to the right buyer, on the right terms, with discretion. This is precisely the dynamic that creates off-market acquisition opportunities for qualified foreign buyers who can be introduced through the right channels.

body.heading: Can Foreigners Buy Swiss Companies?
body.text: Yes: with important nuances. Unlike Swiss real estate (which is regulated by the Lex Koller for foreign buyers), there is no general restriction on foreign ownership of Swiss operating companies. A foreign individual, family office, or corporate entity can acquire 100% of a Swiss GmbH or AG. However, certain regulated sectors, banking, insurance, media, carry additional licensing and approval requirements. And practically speaking, Swiss sellers are highly selective about who they sell to, particularly in succession situations. Trust, cultural fit, and professional credibility matter as much as price.

body.heading: The Role of Swiss Legal Structure
bullets: Swiss AG (Aktiengesellschaft): the Swiss joint-stock company, equivalent to a UK plc or German AG. Minimum share capital CHF 100,000. Most common for larger SMEs and companies with multiple shareholders.
Swiss GmbH (Gesellschaft mit beschränkter Haftung), the Swiss limited liability company. Minimum share capital CHF 20,000. Most common for smaller owner-operated businesses.

Share deal vs. asset deal: most Swiss SME acquisitions are structured as share deals (buyer acquires the company entity). Asset deals are less common but used in specific restructuring or carve-out situations.

Due diligence. Swiss commercial law provides strong protections for buyers, but diligence standards vary significantly for privately-held companies. Independent financial, legal, and tax due diligence is essential.

body.heading: What Foreign Buyers Typically Underestimate
body.text: The Swiss M&A process moves on Swiss time, which is methodical, relationship-driven, and often slower than buyers from the US, Middle East, or Asia expect. Sellers, particularly in succession situations, often spend months or years evaluating potential buyers before formal negotiations begin. Being introduced through a trusted intermediary, rather than cold outreach or an online M&A platform, significantly compresses this timeline and increases the probability of success. Language is also relevant: German-speaking Switzerland (Zurich, Zug, Basel, Lucerne) is the dominant SME market, and while business English is widely spoken, sellers at the succession stage often prefer to conduct sensitive conversations in German or Swiss German.

body.heading: How Global Key Partners Supports Foreign Acquirers
body.text: Global Key Partners acts as a trusted field partner for qualified foreign buyers seeking off-market Swiss business acquisition opportunities. We maintain active relationships with Swiss business owners, advisors, and professional networks, and facilitate introductions on a confidential, mandate-specific basis. We do not list businesses for sale. We do not run auction processes. We connect the right buyers with the right opportunities, at the right time.

body.heading: Typical Acquisition Profile Global Key Partners Works With
bullets: Acquisition size: CHF 2M: CHF 50M enterprise value
Sectors: precision manufacturing, professional services, technology, healthcare, hospitality, speciality retail
Buyer profile: strategic acquirers, family offices, UHNWI principals, international holding companies
Transaction type: full acquisition, majority stake, or structured succession with the existing owner

faqs.q: Can a foreign company or individual acquire 100% of a Swiss SME?
faqs.a: Yes. There is no general restriction on foreign ownership of Swiss operating companies. A foreign individual, family office, or corporate entity can acquire 100% of a Swiss GmbH or AG. Regulated sectors such as banking and insurance carry additional licensing requirements, but most SME sectors are unrestricted.

faqs.q: How do I find off-market Swiss businesses for sale?
faqs.a: Off-market Swiss businesses, particularly in succession situations, are typically accessed through trusted intermediaries with established relationships among Swiss business owners and their advisors. They do not appear on public M&A platforms. GKP facilitates introductions to off-market acquisition opportunities on a confidential, mandate-specific basis.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a Swiss AG and a Swiss GmbH?
faqs.a: A Swiss AG (Aktiengesellschaft) is a joint-stock company with a minimum share capital of CHF 100,000, governed by a board of directors, and typically used for larger businesses or those with multiple shareholders. A Swiss GmbH (Gesellschaft mit beschränkter Haftung) has a minimum share capital of CHF 20,000 and is the most common structure for smaller owner-operated businesses. Both can be fully foreign-owned.

faqs.q: How long does a Swiss SME acquisition typically take?
faqs.a: A Swiss SME acquisition, particularly in a succession situation, typically takes 6–18 months from first introduction to closing. The process is relationship-driven and moves methodically. Being introduced through a trusted intermediary rather than cold outreach can significantly compress the pre-negotiation phase.

faqs.q: Does GKP advise on Swiss business acquisitions for foreign buyers?
faqs.a: GKP acts as a trusted access partner: we source off-market acquisition opportunities and facilitate introductions between qualified foreign buyers and Swiss business owners. We work on a mandate-specific basis and connect clients with appropriate legal, tax, and financial advisors for the transaction itself.

en.title: Switzerland Lump-Sum Taxation: What UHNWI and Family Offices Need to Know | GKP
en.h1: Switzerland Lump-Sum Taxation: What UHNWI and Family Offices Need to Know
en.meta_description: A clear guide to Swiss lump-sum taxation (Pauschalbesteuerung) for ultra-high-net-worth individuals and family offices considering Swiss fiscal domicile, eligibility, cantons, costs, and how it compares globally.
en.summary: Switzerland's lump-sum tax regime is one of the most attractive fiscal arrangements available to UHNWI globally, but it is widely misunderstood. This guide explains who qualifies, how it works, which cantons offer the most favourable terms, and what it means for family offices considering Swiss domicile.

body.text: This article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute tax, legal, or investment advice. Readers should consult a qualified Swiss tax advisor, lawyer, or financial professional before making any decisions.

Switzerland's lump-sum tax regime, known in German as Pauschalbesteuerung, and in French as imposition d'après la dépense, is one of the world's most established and legitimate preferential tax arrangements for ultra-high-net-worth individuals. It has existed in Swiss law since 1862 and continues to attract significant private wealth from across Europe, the Middle East, Asia, and beyond. Yet it remains poorly understood among those it could most benefit.

body.heading: What Is the Swiss Lump-Sum Tax?
body.text: Under the lump-sum regime, a qualifying foreign national who establishes Swiss fiscal domicile pays tax not on their actual worldwide income and assets, but on a notional amount based on their Swiss living expenses. This notional amount is negotiated with the cantonal tax authority and must meet certain minimums set by federal and cantonal law. In practice, this means that a UHNWI with significant global income, from investments, business interests, royalties, or capital gains, may pay Swiss tax on a fraction of what a standard Swiss resident would pay on equivalent wealth.

body.heading: Who Qualifies?
bullets: Foreign nationals only. Swiss citizens cannot access the lump-sum regime regardless of time abroad
First-time Swiss residents, or those who have not been Swiss tax-resident in the 10 years prior
Individuals who do not carry out any gainful activity in Switzerland, the lump-sum regime is not available to those working in Switzerland
The regime extends to the spouse and dependent family members of the qualifying individual

body.heading: How Is the Tax Calculated?
body.text: The taxable base is calculated as the higher of: (1) five times the annual rental cost of the individual's Swiss residence (or the rental value if they own it), and (2) the cantonal and federal minimum thresholds. Federal law sets a minimum taxable base of CHF 400,000 for federal tax purposes. Cantonal minimums vary: some major wealth cantons set their own floors, and the effective tax liability depends heavily on which canton the individual chooses to reside in.

body.heading: Which Cantons Are Most Attractive?
bullets: Valais: historically one of the most favourable cantons for lump-sum taxation; home to many UHNWI residents in resort areas including Verbier and Zermatt
Graubünden: attractive terms; St. Moritz and the Engadin valley have significant UHNWI communities
Vaud. Geneva's neighbouring canton; attractive for those wanting proximity to Geneva's international infrastructure without Geneva's higher tax burden
Obwalden and Nidwalden: central Swiss cantons with competitive cantonal tax rates
Note: the canton of Zurich abolished its cantonal lump-sum regime in 2009; Basel-City and Schaffhausen followed. The regime remains available in the majority of Swiss cantons.

body.heading: What Does It Cost in Practice?
body.text: Effective total tax liability under the Swiss lump-sum regime depends on the canton, the negotiated taxable base, and the applicable rates. As a rough illustration: a qualifying individual residing in Valais, with a negotiated taxable base of CHF 1.5M, might face an effective total Swiss tax liability, cantonal, municipal, and federal, in the range of CHF 350,000–500,000 per year. This is regardless of whether their global income or wealth is CHF 20M, CHF 100M, or more. For context, the UK's non-dom regime, historically the closest global equivalent, was significantly curtailed in 2025, making Switzerland comparatively more attractive.

body.heading: Family Office Implications
body.text: Switzerland's lump-sum regime is frequently the starting point for family office relocation discussions. Once a UHNWI principal establishes Swiss fiscal domicile, the question of where to domicile the family office structure itself follows naturally. Switzerland offers several competitive family office structuring options: the Swiss AG or GmbH as a family holding, Swiss private foundation structures, and access to Switzerland's extensive double-tax treaty network (over 100 treaties) for international income flows.

body.heading: How Global Key Partners Fits In
body.text: GKP is not a tax advisor: lump-sum applications require specialist Swiss tax counsel, cantonal negotiation, and legal structuring that is outside GKP's scope. What Global Key Partners does is connect qualified individuals and family offices who are exploring Swiss domicile with the right specialists, and, once the fiscal decision is made, provide access to off-market real estate, business opportunities, and the private networks that make Swiss residency genuinely valuable beyond the tax advantage.

faqs.q: Can a Singapore or UAE national access Swiss lump-sum taxation?
faqs.a: Yes. The Swiss lump-sum regime is available to any foreign national establishing Swiss fiscal domicile for the first time (or after a 10-year absence), regardless of their country of origin. Singapore and UAE nationals are among those who regularly access the regime.

faqs.q: Is Swiss lump-sum taxation legal and OECD-compliant?
faqs.a: Yes. The Swiss lump-sum tax regime is a fully legal, codified provision of Swiss federal and cantonal tax law. It has been subject to parliamentary scrutiny, public referenda (confirmed by Swiss voters in 2014), and is not on any OECD blacklist. Switzerland has extensive international tax treaties and participates in the Common Reporting Standard (CRS).

faqs.q: How does Swiss lump-sum tax compare to UK non-dom status?
faqs.a: The UK's non-domiciled resident regime was significantly restricted in April 2025, making Switzerland comparatively more attractive for UHNWI considering European fiscal domicile. The Swiss regime offers a clear, negotiated annual tax liability with no time limit, while the reformed UK regime now taxes worldwide income after a period of UK residence.

faqs.q: Can I work in Switzerland if I am on the lump-sum tax regime?
faqs.a: No. The lump-sum regime is explicitly restricted to individuals who carry out no gainful activity in Switzerland. Managing passive investments and family wealth does not constitute a gainful activity, but employment, a Swiss board seat with remuneration, or an active Swiss business role would disqualify the individual from the regime.

faqs.q: Does GKP advise on Swiss lump-sum tax applications?
faqs.a: GKP is not a tax advisor. We connect qualified individuals exploring Swiss fiscal domicile with specialist Swiss tax counsel and cantonal advisors, and once the fiscal decision is made, we provide access to off-market real estate, private networks, and investment opportunities in Switzerland.

en.title: Hospitality Investment in Switzerland: A Private Investor's Guide to Boutique Hotels & Alpine Resorts | GKP
en.h1: Hospitality Investment in Switzerland: A Private Investor's Guide to Boutique Hotels and Alpine Resorts
en.meta_description: A practical guide to hospitality investment in Switzerland, covering boutique hotel and alpine resort acquisitions, off-market access, structuring, Lex Koller considerations, and what qualified private investors should know.
en.summary: Switzerland's hospitality investment market offers private investors a rare combination: hard real estate assets, operational income, personal use value, and exposure to one of the world's most resilient luxury tourism markets. This guide covers what private investors and family offices need to know before entering the Swiss hospitality investment market — from boutique hotels to alpine resorts.

body.text: This article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute tax, legal, or investment advice. Readers should consult a qualified Swiss tax advisor, lawyer, or financial professional before making any decisions.

Swiss alpine hospitality is a category of its own. The country's reputation for precision, discretion, and natural beauty has supported a luxury tourism industry that has remained remarkably resilient across economic cycles. For private investors and family offices, Swiss boutique hotels and alpine resort assets represent a distinctive investment thesis, combining the characteristics of prime real estate with the income dynamics of a hospitality business, in one of Europe's most stable jurisdictions.

body.heading: The Swiss Hospitality Investment Landscape
body.text: Switzerland's hotel market is dominated by independent and family-owned properties, particularly in the alpine resort tier. The large international chains have a presence in major cities. Zurich, Geneva, Basel, but the alpine resort market (Zermatt, Verbier, St. Moritz, Gstaad, Engelberg, Davos) remains largely in the hands of private owners, many of whom have operated the same property for generations. This creates a structurally off-market investment environment: when ownership changes, it happens through private networks, not public listings or formal sale processes.

body.heading: Why Private Investors Are Attracted to Swiss Hospitality
bullets: Hard asset stability. Swiss alpine real estate has appreciated consistently over decades; the underlying land and building value provides a floor that purely operational hospitality assets lack
CHF-denominated income. Swiss franc revenues provide a natural hedge for investors holding significant CHF or EUR-denominated wealth
Personal use value: ownership of a resort or boutique hotel in the Swiss Alps carries lifestyle and personal use value that institutional investors cannot price but private investors can
Resilient demand. Swiss alpine tourism draws from a global UHNWI and luxury traveller base that has proven relatively recession-resistant

Succession dynamics: many family-owned Swiss alpine hotels face the same succession wave as Swiss SMEs generally, creating opportunities for acquisition at reasonable valuations without a competitive auction

body.heading: Lex Koller and Swiss Hotel Investment
body.text: The Lex Koller. Switzerland's law restricting foreign ownership of Swiss residential real estate, has specific implications for hospitality investment that are frequently misunderstood. Hotel properties classified as commercial real estate are generally not subject to Lex Koller restrictions and can be purchased freely by foreign investors. However, mixed-use properties, where a hotel contains residential apartments or chalets sold to end-users, can trigger Lex Koller considerations depending on the legal classification of each unit. Purpose-built resort residences sold to foreign buyers in designated tourist zones are subject to specific cantonal quotas. GKP works with specialist legal advisors to navigate structuring from the outset.

body.heading: Typical Deal Structures
bullets: Full acquisition: purchase of the operating company (share deal) or the real estate and business assets (asset deal); most common for boutique hotels and smaller alpine properties

Majority stake with continuing owner: particularly relevant in succession situations where the founding family wishes to retain a minority interest and operational involvement during a transition period
Co-investment alongside an experienced operator, family offices sometimes prefer to invest alongside a proven hospitality operator rather than take on direct operational responsibility
Sale-and-leaseback: current owner sells the real estate to an investor and leases it back on a long-term basis; provides capital to the seller while generating a stable yield for the buyer

body.heading: Key Markets Within Switzerland
bullets: Zermatt: car-free, highly regulated, one of the world's most iconic alpine destinations; property ownership is restricted to Swiss nationals and residents, making Lex Koller navigation essential
Verbier: international and cosmopolitan; significant British, Middle Eastern, and international UHNWI ownership; relatively more accessible for foreign investors
St. Moritz: the original luxury alpine resort; Graubünden canton; established UHNWI community; boutique and historic hotel properties occasionally available
Grindelwald and Engelberg: growing in international profile; more accessible price points than the top-tier resorts; strong year-round tourism
Lucerne region: four-season city tourism; strong Asian visitor base; boutique and lifestyle hotel assets with urban-resort crossover characteristics

body.heading: How Global Key Partners Sources Hospitality Opportunities
body.text: GKP maintains active relationships with Swiss alpine hotel owners, family holding companies, cantonal tourism bodies, and specialist hospitality advisors. When ownership transitions arise, often years before any formal sale process, we are positioned to facilitate confidential introductions to qualified buyers and co-investors. We do not list properties publicly. Every introduction is made on a mandate-specific, confidential basis.

body.heading: Southeast Asia Connection
body.text: GKP's network spans both Swiss alpine hospitality and Southeast Asian resort markets, including boutique hotel and resort assets in Thailand and Malaysia. For investors interested in both corridors. Swiss alpine in winter, Southeast Asian resort in summer. GKP is uniquely positioned to facilitate access across both geographies through a single trusted relationship.

faqs.q: Can foreign investors buy hotels in Switzerland?
faqs.a: Yes. Hotel properties classified as commercial real estate in Switzerland are generally not subject to Lex Koller restrictions and can be purchased freely by foreign investors. Mixed-use or residential resort properties may carry restrictions depending on cantonal classification. GKP connects investors with specialist legal advisors for structuring from the outset.

faqs.q: What is the typical investment size for a Swiss boutique hotel acquisition?
faqs.a: Swiss boutique hotel acquisitions typically range from CHF 3M for smaller alpine guesthouses to CHF 30M+ for established resort properties in prime locations. Properties in Zermatt, Verbier, and St. Moritz command the highest values; secondary alpine markets offer more accessible entry points with strong tourism fundamentals.

faqs.q: Are Swiss alpine hotel investments subject to the Lex Koller?
faqs.a: Hotel properties classified as commercial real estate are generally exempt from Lex Koller restrictions. However, resort developments that include residential components sold to foreign buyers may trigger cantonal quota systems. GKP works with specialist legal counsel to clarify the classification and structuring implications for each specific property.

faqs.q: Does GKP have access to off-market Swiss hotel and resort investment opportunities?
faqs.a: Yes. GKP maintains active relationships with Swiss alpine hotel owners, family holding companies, and specialist hospitality networks. We facilitate confidential introductions to off-market hospitality investment opportunities, boutique hotels, alpine resorts, and lifestyle hospitality assets, on a mandate-specific basis.

faqs.q: Can GKP help with hospitality investments in Southeast Asia as well as Switzerland?
faqs.a: Yes. GKP's network spans both Swiss alpine hospitality and Southeast Asian resort markets, including boutique hotel and resort assets in Thailand and Malaysia. We facilitate access across both geographies for investors interested in diversified hospitality portfolios.

en.title: Taiwan Family Offices Investing in Switzerland: A Practical Guide
en.meta_description: A guide for Taiwanese family offices and UHNWI on investing in Switzerland: real estate, business acquisitions, Lex Koller, and how to access off-market opportunities.
en.intro: Taiwan's family offices and high-net-worth entrepreneurs are increasingly looking beyond Asia for long-term capital preservation. Switzerland offers what few markets can match: political neutrality, a strong legal framework, deep private banking tradition, and an off-market deal ecosystem that rewards relationships over scale.

body.text: This article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute tax, legal, or investment advice. Readers should consult a qualified Swiss tax advisor, lawyer, or financial professional before making any decisions.

Taiwan has produced one of Asia's most concentrated pools of private wealth, built through decades of technology, manufacturing, and family enterprise. As second and third generation family members take on stewardship roles, international diversification has become a defining priority. Switzerland is emerging as a preferred destination.
Why Swiss Assets Appeal to Taiwanese Investors

The Swiss franc is one of the world's most stable reserve currencies. Swiss real estate, particularly residential and commercial property in Zurich, Geneva, and Zug, has delivered consistent long-term returns with low volatility. For Taiwanese family offices managing multigenerational wealth, that combination is compelling.

Beyond real estate, Switzerland offers access to private business acquisitions. Swiss SMEs, often owner-managed, profitable, and discretely available, represent a category of investment that rarely appears on public markets. For Taiwanese principals with operational backgrounds, acquiring a Swiss business can also open doors to the European single market and Swiss residency pathways.
Lex Koller: What Taiwanese Investors Need to Know

Switzerland's Lex Koller legislation restricts the purchase of residential real estate by non-residents from outside the EU/EFTA. Taiwanese nationals without Swiss residency are subject to these restrictions and will need to structure acquisitions carefully, typically through a Swiss-domiciled entity or via commercial property, which is exempt.

Commercial real estate, business acquisitions, and investments through Swiss legal structures are generally not affected. GKP works with legal counsel to identify the right structure before any transaction begins.
Lump-Sum Taxation for Taiwanese Relocating to Switzerland

Switzerland's lump-sum tax regime (Pauschalbesteuerung) is available to foreign nationals who take up Swiss residency without pursuing employment in Switzerland. For Taiwanese UHNWI considering a relocation, whether for lifestyle, education, or tax planning, Swiss lump-sum taxation offers a globally competitive structure negotiated directly with the cantonal tax authority.
How Global Key Partners Works with Taiwanese Capital

GKP operates exclusively in Switzerland. We source off-market real estate and business opportunities, introduce Taiwanese investors to Swiss principals, and provide end-to-end support from first approach through to closing. Our process is discreet, relationship-driven, and tailored to each mandate.
We do not list opportunities publicly. If you are a Taiwanese family office or private investor considering Switzerland, GKP welcomes a direct conversation.

faqs.a: Off-market Swiss real estate transactions GKP advises on typically start at CHF 2 million. Premium residential and commercial assets in Zurich and Geneva often range from CHF 5–20 million and above.
